output.summary_final: {"title": "Building Trails That Last and Riding Smarter", "bullets": ["Speed often comes from braking earlier and smoother rather than taking bigger risks on the trail.", "On berms, keep eyes through the exit, hips centered, and build pressure gradually instead of spiking.", "Tire pressure and casing choice frequently matter more than making minor suspension tweaks.", "Well-draining trails stay rideable longer and maintain soil structure instead of turning into ruts.", "Most mystery creaks are solved by cleaning contact surfaces, re-greasing threads, and re-torquing to spec.", "A good maintenance routine is short and boring: check bolts, measure chain wear, clean stanchions, and inspect pads.", "The bike should feel quiet and predictable so attention stays on the trail rather than noises or surprises.", "Riders improve faster by repeating one trail segment and changing only one variable at a time.", "For climbs, a consistent cadence reduces drivetrain load and makes small traction changes easier to manage.", "In wet conditions, the best skill is decision-making: knowing when to back off to protect traction and trails."]}

{"title": "Building Sustainable On-Call and System Reliability", "bullets": ["Design on-call rotations so waking up is rare and responses are obvious when incidents occur.", "Handle secrets with short-lived, narrowly scoped tokens rotated automatically to improve security.", "Treat failures as expected events and contain them using timeouts, retries, and clear ownership.", "Keep threat modeling lightweight by identifying assets, entry points, trust boundaries, and likely abuse cases.", "Structure RFCs with context, constraints, options, and a recommendation that explicitly outlines risks.", "Ensure error budgets drive behavioral changes rather than serving as ignored charts during planning.", "Make alerting action-oriented; if no immediate action exists, the alert should not trigger a page.", "Prioritize security by making the secure path the easiest option instead of relying on shame or heroics."]}

{"title": "Wreck Diving Fundamentals: Safety, Navigation, and Buoyancy", "bullets": ["Maintaining calm is critical for safety as it slows breathing and enables deliberate decision-making under pressure.", "Effective navigation requires selecting one primary reference and one backup, such as heading plus depth or reef contour.", "Carrying a backup light provides mental reassurance and changes a diver's state by ensuring options exist during emergencies.", "Good buoyancy control protects the environment while reducing task load for navigation and photography inside wrecks.", "Stress underwater often stems from surprise and lack of rehearsal rather than the inherent difficulty of the problem.", "The ability to call a dive early without ego is the most valuable skill for ensuring diver safety and managing risk.", "Simple dive plans should be shared and include clear turn pressures and defined exit routes to prevent confusion.", "Neutral buoyancy drills like hovering and slow fin pivots make managing choppy conditions significantly easier."]}

{"title": "Mastering Underwater Photography: Lighting, Positioning, and Workflow", "bullets": ["Great underwater images stem from a clear concept and a simple setup executed consistently rather than complex gear.", "Adjusting the strobe angle away from the lens axis dramatically reduces backscatter by moving the light beam.", "Lighting is fundamentally about control, dictating where shadows fall, managing spill, and directing viewer focus.", "Using a grid on lights is primarily for controlling spill and keeping light off dark areas, not just for softness.", "In mixed lighting scenarios, decide on a dominant color temperature first and correct all other elements relative to that choice.", "Documentary work relies on strict routines like backups, notes, and small habits to prevent catastrophic data loss.", "Labeling files and writing captions immediately after shooting prevents memory gaps that can ruin long-term projects.", "Practicing with a single light teaches more about placement than a complicated kit, as the camera is rarely the limiting factor."]}

{"title": "Index Investing Without the Myths", "bullets": ["Risk is defined as the chance of not meeting goals or suffering permanent loss, distinct from simple market volatility.", "Index investing succeeds by reducing costs and decision errors rather than predicting future market movements.", "Position sizing rules are essential to prevent a single great idea from becoming a portfolio-ending mistake.", "Fees compound quietly over time, so lowering costs effectively increases returns without taking additional risk.", "True diversification requires assets with different drivers, not just different tickers that move in tandem.", "Behavioral consistency is the hardest challenge, requiring investors to stay the course when headlines are loud.", "A 50% portfolio loss requires a 100% gain to recover, significantly altering investment timelines.", "Real estate appears stable until leverage, vacancy, and maintenance issues converge simultaneously.", "Investors should stress test their ability to handle higher rates and repairs without panic selling."]}
